aliases: Mutton in Cream and Curd Gravy

english: Mutton in Cream and Curd Gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Gazala Gosht or Afghani mutton curry; FOODON_03315671 (mutton dish) used as the closest broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Mutton meat dish) is the closest defensible broader category; no specific Afghani mutton curry code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept identified for Gazala Gosht or Afghani mutton curry at this time.

preparation_method: simmered in cream and curd sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Gazala Gosht is approximately 300 g, comprising mutton pieces in a semi-thick cream-and-curd-based Afghani gravy, usually served with naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton curry (with bone), cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for cooked mutton curry (with bone) used as the primary anchor. Cream and yogurt enrichment characteristic of Afghani-style preparation increases fat and energy slightly; values adjusted upward by approximately 10–15% for fat and energy to reflect the cream-and-curd base. Protein and carbohydrate values retained from ICMR-NIN mutton curry baseline. This is an adapted estimate; exact restaurant formulation is unknown.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Meat-Based Curry Dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate meat curry reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gazala Gosht. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of meat-based curries with minimal carbohydrate content. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with very low carbohydrate (3.2 g/100 g), consistent with low-GI meat curry analogues. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 × 3.2 × 3) / 100 = 2.88, rounded to 2.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg/100 g; ~1140 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Gazala Gosht provides approximately 1140 mg sodium, representing nearly 50% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~30)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Gazala Gosht has a low glycemic index (~30) and very low carbohydrate content, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 2.9 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream, yogurt, and mutton (~4.8 g/100 g; ~14.4 g per serving) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The cream-and-curd base contributes approximately 14.4 g saturated fat per 300 g serving, exceeding the 10–13 g daily saturated fat limit recommended for cardiovascular risk reduction; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gazala Gosht (Semi Gravy) is a North Indian and Afghani-inspired mutton curry dish characterised by a rich, semi-thick sauce made from cream (malai) and yogurt (dahi), seasoned with black pepper, garam masala, and chaat masala. The dish features slow-cooked or braised mutton pieces — typically bone-in — that absorb the creamy, mildly spiced gravy during cooking. The flavour profile is mellow and aromatic rather than fiery, with the cream and yogurt lending a tangy richness and the black pepper providing gentle heat. The texture is tender mutton in a velvety, semi-thick sauce that clings to the meat. In Singapore, this dish is served at North Indian restaurants and is associated with the Afghani cuisine tradition popularised in the Indian subcontinent. Nutritionally, Gazala Gosht is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with very low carbohydrate content (~3.2 g/100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The cream and yogurt base contributes saturated fat (~4.8 g/100 g) and calcium. Sodium content is elevated (~380 mg/100 g) due to salt and spice blends used in restaurant preparation. The glycemic index is low (~30), and the glycemic load per standard 300 g serving is approximately 2.9, making it a favourable choice for individuals managing blood glucose. Clinically, the primary concerns are sodium load for hypertensive individuals and saturated fat intake for those with cardiovascular risk. It is typically served with naan, roti, or steamed rice.
common_user_queries: Is Gazala Gosht good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Gazala Gosht and regular mutton curry?
North Indian mutton curry with cream

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Gazala Gosht exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore databases. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked mutton curry data with upward adjustment for cream and yogurt enrichment characteristic of Afghani-style preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database meat curry references. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked mutton curry (with bone) baseline, adjusted upward by approximately 10–15% for fat and energy to reflect the cream-and-curd Afghani-style preparation. Exact restaurant formulation varies across the three Singapore restaurants identified in the source data (Curry Village, Bombay Curry And Kebabs, Aryana). Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN mutton curry source entry. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN mutton curry source entry. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN mutton curry source entry. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Gazala Gosht was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (North Indian Muslim cuisine tradition) but is not independently verified. Price range SGD 19.90–23.90 per portion as observed on field survey data.
